speaker: interviewer
text: September 1995. Edith, we've got to the point where you were coming to the end of your stay in Theresienstadt. Do you want to tell us what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes, well, in the-- before the transports started arriving from the death marches, we still thought that most of the people who went east would survive. But once we learned the truth, we lost all hope that we'll see our families again. And when I went to Prague, and I got to Prague, for a while I stayed with my aunt, out, out of town, in Brandys. And then I realized I had to go to Prague to try to claim our property and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with an old school friend, non-Jewish school friend. And I really thought they were fantastic. Because I told them I might be carrying typhus. And they said, well, they'd take the risk. And they accommodated. They fed me. And-- Wow, that's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: They let, let me stay there. So that was really fantastic. And [SIGHS] I sort of tried to get our flat back, which was our own unit. But a policeman was already living there. And there was no way I could get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I queued up at the local council. And they said, well, they don't know that my mother survived. I had no proof. And they can't give me, I'm only a young girl, and-- How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19. And I-- you see, the people who had a bit more experience or a bit more brain than I, they got big flats in the good quarters. And I just wanted to go where I was used to living, which wasn't quite as-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] quite as good. And finally, I managed, I managed to get a small, very nice flat. But then two men came and pushed me out of it. So then I had to go again and queue up. And then I got a little mansard, sort of just a bachelor, one room, one big room, not even--it didn't even have a kitchen, it had a little kitchenette and was on the fourth floor and no lift. And it was rather primitive. So I was worried about the rent, that I wouldn't be able to pay the rent. Because I didn't know how long my mother would stay in Theresienstadt. I had no income, and I just, I just didn't know. I realized I had no money, no, no profession, no clothes, no, no nothing, no family, no nothing. And [SIGHS] then the headmaster of my high school sent two former school friends of mine to find me. They had big lists at the police stations of who came back. We used to scan them every day, to see whether anybody came back we knew. And they found that I have returned. And they-- the headmaster summoned them to ask me to come for an interview. So I thought it was rather silly. I have missed so much schooling, and I'd have to earn money. I didn't know what I'd live on. And he said they, they decided-- they had some money. They collected some money, and they established a special fund. And I should come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to my original class. Of course, the boys have changed from when there were 12. All of a sudden they were men. And the girls were young ladies. And I went back to my old class. And the boys were sailing paper airplanes, and I thought, I thought, [SIGHS] you know, time has stopped for them or something, I, I-- You'd gone through so many changes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt so ancient. And all the boys wanted to know how many times I've been raped. But that was the only thing that interested them somehow. And so I went back to the headmaster. I said, look, [SIGHS] they're doing higher mathematics. I haven't-- I haven't got a clue. And he says, you'll catch up, you'll catch up. And in the school holidays, he said, you can have as many books as you want to. And you do your exams, pass each year. And you'll, I'm sure you'll get to matriculation next year. And I got a job in a-- there was a convalescent-- there was some Czech charitable organization confiscated some castles. And they made convalescent home from-- for children from concentration camps. And they were getting better food, of course, from America. The UNRA was sending some food there. I got a job as a gardener. And I worked from 6 to 12, and then the rest of the day was mine. I had my own little room. And I started. And my brain was so good, it was incredible. I, I just could study so well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I matriculated with my class. So I did very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, had she come back by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then my mother came back. And she found out where I was. And she came to visit me. And then, in the meantime, then she went back to stay at the flat. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she stayed at the flat you found?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found, yeah. I could tell her where it was and give her the key. She learned where I was. And then one day, I was coming-- already back in Prague-- I was coming home in the evening. And all of a sudden, a man in an army uniform stands up, and I, I had a dreadful shock. But it was my cousin [LAUGHS] who came back with the British army. So that was quite-- you see, somehow news traveled. And you got to know who came, and where they lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I-- there were soup kitchens, and we used to go around to the various soup kitchens, always three or four every day. Because we were always hungry. And that's where you met people. And you sort of asked, you know, what happened to so-and-so? And you asked, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a very good friend. We went-- we shared everything in camp. She had a sister, and the mother, father was in England. [SIGHS] And they shot her on the last day of the war. They went on a hunger march. And her younger sister couldn't walk anymore, and she tried to support her. And they shot them both. And so they-- And when I was guiding during the exhibition, 'The Children of the Holocaust,' one of the pictures was done by the little sister, by Sonia, and it-- oh, it was quite dreadful to see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, amazing. And so you got you matriculation.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back. I matriculated, and I got a job. A fellow who was in Theresienstadt also, he sort of heard that I was looking for a job. So he offered me a job as a, as what one used to call a correspondent, a secretary, really, a bilingual or trilingual secretary. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you type?

speaker: interviewee
text: I taught myself to type and shorthand in the school-- in the holidays. And I was a bit slow. But it improved. And I did that for a while. And then I had an offer to become secretary to the director of HIAS, the Hebrew Immigrant Aid Society. So I was there for a year and a half. And that's how I managed to go to Australia. Because they sponsored the action for young girls to go to Australia. And I, I managed to get a permit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you choose Australia? Did you have any connections here?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I knew nobody. I had two choices, Canada or Australia. And I decided, if I'm going to be hungry, it's easier in a warm climate. [LAUGHTER] Besides, I felt I've been cold enough during the war, and I wanted to be warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your mother going to go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she couldn't get a permit, because she was over 50. Australia, at the time, would not admit anybody who was sick or anything like that, and over 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it was the same in South Africa then.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came here on my own. And then I was able to-- I had to have somebody to guarantee for my mother. Well, how do you-- and it had to be a household, somebody who owned property, a property owner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now how-- I didn't know anybody who owned property. How, how do you get a guarantee? And how would you get anybody to guarantee for a person they don't know? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, it's-- so finally, somebody knows somebody. And then two families gave me the guarantee. If they're Jewish, a welfare society would co-guarantee.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got it finally together. I managed to send my mother landing permit. And two years after I arrived, my mother came. So she must have been very devastated when you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Not knowing whether she was going to join you or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was, she was dead against it. But I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that must have taken quite a lot of courage on your part to make that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, meanwhile the Communists have taken over in '48. And I decided, I don't want to be afraid if-- ever again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was looking for ways of leaving. My mother didn't want to leave, because she had a little pension there. She had a little flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a few pupils, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: She could lead a stable life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she didn't want to. And then when I got the permit-- and I didn't tell her anything. I got my passport, and I did my-- get my visas and everything. And I only told her right at the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you left?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was awfully against it. And meanwhile, I had a boyfriend who was also awfully against it. But I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left him in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Czechoslovakia. So when you came here, you knew hardly anybody. Nobody. And where did you first--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was rather funny. I, the people I-- the person I've worked for, whose secretary I was, my first job-- he went to Australia, while I was at the Hebrew Aid Society. And I was in charge of transportation at the time. And somebody dubbed him in that he tried to get some money out or whatever. And he-- somebody told him they were going to fetch him that night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so he came in desperation to my office, said, can you do anything for me? Can you get me two seats? We've got passports. Can you get us two seats on the train out? And so I said I'd try my best. And I did get them two seats. And he said, Edith, if you ever come to Australia, our home is your home. I said, what will I ever do in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sort of forgot all about it. And then, when I knew I was coming here, I had the address. The lady's brother was in Australia, and I had his address, because when they left, they gave me his address. So I wrote to him that I was coming. I heard nothing anymore, so. When I arrived, the ship docked finally in Australia. And everybody had relations. And I was very depressed. I sat on my bunk, and I was very depressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What will I do? Where will I go? The Jewish welfare didn't come on, on the ship. There was nobody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the steward came and said, there's somebody waiting for you downstairs. I said, nobody knows. I don't know anybody, must be a mistake. And then he comes again, said, look, it is for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went down, and there was the brother with the car to fetch me. And I-- Oh, amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I lived with those people for about two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: How incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- which was really wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, incredible. And so how old were you when you came? You were 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was 1940--

speaker: interviewer
text: 9, right. And you came to Sydney. To Sydney. I decided Sydney had the better climate. That must have been quite primitive here when you got here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a shock, all the little-- town was all those little two-story houses and no big--

speaker: interviewer
text: Buildings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Buildings, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no, like you were used to, the restaurants and cafes. And nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. And the bread was delivered on the steps unwrapped, you know, just a brick of the bread. And you put a tin out for milk. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite amazing. And everybody, all the old ladies-- what I considered old, the 40-year-olds, probably-- had pink or, or pale blue hats and gloves. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what suburb did you live in when you first got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- these people lived in Artarmon, so I lived in Artarmon. And then, when my mother came, I managed to get a, a room under a house in Chatswood. It was a laundry And the room, you know, it was a house on a slope. And we had the room underneath. It was rather funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gentleman, when he interviewed me, said, what radio station do you listen to? I said, 2BL. He said, you've got it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was awfully difficult to get even a room. People queued up at the at 3 in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing. Was it lack of accommodations?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- no, because all the migrants were coming. And you couldn't, couldn't get anything. And so I was very lucky that I was staying with the Gregoras. And then I got-- friends of ours had this little flat, had. And then they moved to something better. So they recommended us to these people. And we got that. And we lived there for two years, till I got married, and we bought a flat in Bronte. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a bushwalking club. [LAUGHS] There was a partly European-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], which meant, I think, Aboriginal for friendship. And they also attended cultural activities, concerts, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was going on a Christmas camp, and there was this guy I didn't know. And they said, oh, didn't you meet Walter Sheldon? He is a new member. And you got on straight away?

speaker: interviewee
text: He carried my suitcase all the way up the hill. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: '52, January '52. We went out together for a year. And then we married January '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where had he immigrated from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was born in Vienna. And in '39, I think, his parents sent him to England, with the Children's Action. His father finished up in Shanghai. And his mother in Auschwitz, where she died in '44. And his grandmother went to Theresienstadt. And my mother actually remembered her quite well. She nursed her, till she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was a pianist. And they somehow had a--

speaker: interviewer
text: That in common.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Mutual interest. And they'd hum tunes to each other and so forth. And so my mother remembered his grandmother very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: How amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his brother and sister also went to England. And they were sent to Australia, early in the war. And my husband was in the north of England, as a agricultural laborer. He never went to school again. A farming family took him in, near Durham. And he lived there. And then, at the end of the war, he decided he wanted to study. So he did the London Matric very quickly and got a place in at the University of Wales. And he studied chemistry-- agricultural chemistry there, because he thought it would be useful in Australia. And he went to Australia. He got a job at the university in biochemistry, in research. And he didn't earn very much, so he went, got a job at [? Torman's. ?] He worked at [? Torman's ?] for a while. And he still hadn't earned very much. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he became a hawker. He saw a lot of people were hawking, selling clothes door-to-door. So that was the stage when I met him. And we used to unpack the suitcases and fold it every day. So direct selling, this was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Direct selling, as you call it. He did that for about six years. And he was always interested in shares. His uncle was a stockbroker, and his father was interested in it. And so eventually, he got in, into stockbroking. And he's still working as a stockbroker, after all those years. 35, 37 years now, he's been in stockbroking. So it's quite a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had children.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have two boys, or two men, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, '55, '58. We lived in Bronte for 10 years. So they went to the primary school for a while there, or rather the elder one. Then we moved here. And then they both went to New Sydney Boys High and to Sydney University after that. And they both studied economics and industrial relations. My elder son lectures at Griffith University in Queensland, in industrial relations and labor history. And my younger son works in a firm, which does environmental impact studies. And he is very active in council, environmental matters, and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are very happy. They've got lovely girls, lovely wives. And our elder son has got two boys. And my younger son, one boy. And do you think that your experiences during the war and Theresienstadt affected your personality--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely, definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel that things don't worry me. I don't worry about little things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Little things, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now always, when my husband was worried, I said, is it a matter of life and death? If it isn't, why are you worried?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You get a good perspective after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sort of you, you know what's important and what isn't important. And I didn't worry when I went for a job interview. Because, well, either I get the job, or I don't get the job, but-- and also human relations became very precious for me, and family, of course. Because our family was so sadly decimated, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lost most of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, my aunt was married to a non-Jew, so that family survived. And my two cousins were in England, so they survived. But the rest, was, everybody was-- nobody came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Killed, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a very tragic-- and, and did you think about why you survived? How you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to feel very guilty about it. We all did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So many died.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, because we just felt that somehow it wasn't fair. Why should I survive and not the others, who were, perhaps, so much more talented and had so much more to give? Also I used to feel guilty for not having been to Auschwitz. Because Theresienstadt wasn't half as bad as the extermination camps. And of course, you get over it  in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But definitely I, I look at things-- material things don't matter. It's, it's the human relations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely. And do you think it affected the way you brought up your sons, with that philosophy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, I think so. I'm pretty sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they share your philosophy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think so. They're not-- not materialistic. And they don't have to have designer jeans or whatever. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, because we live in a very materialistic culture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they are very modest and their wives, also, of the similar ilk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, that's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I don't-- I mean, if you have it, you have it. If you don't have it, you don't have it. But I don't judge people by--

speaker: interviewer
text: What they have.

speaker: interviewee
text: --What they have or what they achieved, whether they've got high position or not. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: The quality of the person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it doesn't, doesn't matter to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And in terms of your work after the Holocaust and here, have you been involved in any Holocaust activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm a voluntary guide at the museum. I was active-- there was a children, I think, I can't remember what it was called, at the Great Synagogue-- there was an exhibition. I was active in that. And then there was the 'Children of the Holocaust,' which was opened at the War Memorial in Canberra, which was quite an unprecedented step for the War Memorial to take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a voluntary guide there. We had, I think, two guides, twice a week. We manned the exhibition. And I was there for the opening. And I was active in that, because I remembered quite a lot, having been there for three years. And also I knew quite a few of the girls that drew those pictures, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was an art exhibition.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a picture-- there is in Prague, there is a collection of drawings, made by the children in Theresienstadt. What happened, the children were not allowed to be taught. That was done secretly. But they were allowed to learn drawing. The Germans permitted that. And at, at some stage there were quite a lot of children. And there was a very famous teacher, Friedl Brandeis. And she taught the children drawing. And she had drawing classes. And the man who was in charge of our barracks kept these drawings and hid them. And after the war, he had two suitcases full of those drawings. He said that was all he managed to bring back from the camp, was two suitcases of drawings. And he gave them to the Prague Jewish Museum. And eventually, they are exhibited. And it's a huge draw card in Prague, in the Jewish-- they get tours to go there and view those pictures. And the, the assistant director of the War Memorial saw them. And he was so impressed that he negotiated for some of the pictures to be shown in Australia. And we had about 40 of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they're not on permanent exhibition here. They were just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were returned again. Except one, there was-- Prime Minister Keating opened the exhibition. And he asked, are there any pictures by anybody who lives in Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was one picture. And that was in Washington. So Prime Minister said we should get it. And it is in Canberra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- they didn't know who it was by. Somebody asked me, at the museum, you know everybody. Do you know anybody called Kitty Passarova? I said, yes, you want her telephone number?

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was the-- you saw the wedding picture. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Kitty's picture. As a child, she drew that picture. And it is exhibited in the War Memorial in Canberra.

speaker: interviewer
text: Canberra, oh, amazing. When they did the exhibition, did they make a book of the drawings? Because sometimes they make a book--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a book in existence of the drawings. I can show you. I've got a book of the drawings.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd be really interested. Because I teach art to children.

speaker: interviewee
text: You teach art to children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, I'd be very interested in that environment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've got, I think, a couple of books. I've got a lot of books on the art and, and the music in the-- during the Holocaust and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you find it a value to be involved in Holocaust-related activities and a guide at the museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the beginning, it upset me a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I feel we are the last ones. And we have to bear witness, if-- you get all sorts of stupid questions when you're there. People think that the photographs are posed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they don't-- we say-- well, I say to people, it's the German, from the German archives. And they say, why would they take photographs like that? I said, because they were enjoyable activities. They enjoyed shooting the Jews, so they took photographs. And it was in the German archives. It's, it's all genuine, from the German archives. There are statistics, there are even train time tables to, to the various extermination camps. And it says that the wagons are not to stop on the way in these and these stops, but go directly. And the weight is so much loaded, and so much empty. I mean, it's all there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Extraordinary.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's very worthwhile to be involved--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: --In doing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. We get a lot of schools. And I think that is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the children need to hear--

speaker: interviewee
text: To hear because lots of the children, A, never met a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And B, never, ever saw a Holocaust survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a long time ago. I remember when my uncles talked about World War I, I thought it's old hat, and that was 20 years later. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or not even that. 15 years later. And I thought it was old hat, and who wants to listen to it? So, I mean, it's similar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So 50 years later now.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, yes. Is there anything else you'd like to add before we look at your photographs? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's about-- I think I'd just like to say I'm very grateful to be in Australia. I think the people here were very friendly and really welcomed us with open arms. [DRINKS] And I felt-- after the war, I felt a certain anti-Semitism, even in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I felt it was absent here. I felt absolutely good here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Comfortable here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's been a good place to have your family and bring up your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thanks very much, Edith.
